CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (Intro → related work → compositional representations → inference → experiments → discussion) and states its main contributions early (introduction clearly presents Edward and its two compositional representations). Notation is generally consistent and defined (e.g., Eq. (1), q(z,β;λ), scale arguments for subsampling), and the many code examples and referenced figures/tables make concepts concrete. Presentation issues are minor but present: several duplicated/garbled paragraphs and sentence repetitions in the latter sections (notably around C.2 and the long repeated blocks) and a few small grammatical/formatting glitches that slightly hinder readability.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a notable and reasonably novel contribution: treating inference as a first-class, compositional representation alongside random variables and integrating both into computational graphs (Introduction and Sec. 3–4). Prior PPLs (Venture/Anglican, WebPPL, Stan, PyMC3) are discussed and the authors claim Edward adds composable inference models, data subsampling, and reuse of model representation within inference (Sec. 2, 4.3–4.4), plus practical GPU-accelerated inference (Sec. 5.2). These are non-trivial extensions over prior work and clearly differentiated in the text, so the contribution is more than incremental but not a radical theoretical breakthrough, hence a 4.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main claims (composition of random variables and inference, and practical flexibility) are well supported by concrete examples (VAE, Bayesian RNN, Dirichlet-process mixture, composable inference routines) and a clear formalization of inference as stochastic graph optimization (Eq. 1). The mathematical content is lightweight (no deep new proofs) and appears correct and consistent. The efficiency claim is backed by an HMC runtime benchmark showing large GPU speedups, but the experimental methodology lacks some controls needed for a fully rigorous comparison (e.g., differing floating-point precision across systems, different core usage, exclusion of compilation time, and no multiple runs or statistical error bars), so the magnitude of the reported speedups should be interpreted cautiously. Overall the technical arguments are sound with only minor experimental-evaluation caveats that do not undermine the main contributions.
